title: PROP-029 'Ok([]) → block-parse' path is unreachable from the real parser; only test stubs exercise it, weakening the verification claim
spec_quote: REQ-017 Edge Cases: 'parseMarkdownToBlocks(snapshot.body) returns Ok([]) (e.g., body containing only whitespace or only YAML-frontmatter delimiters): folds to failure: { kind: \'hydrate\', reason: \'block-parse\' }.' / PROP-029: 'parseMarkdownToBlocks(snapshot.body) returning Ok([]) (e.g., whitespace-only body) is folded by the Step 2 caller to CorruptedFile.failure: { kind: \'hydrate\', reason: \'block-parse\' }.'
issue: PROP-029 (required:true) tests the Ok([]) → block-parse mapping in scan-vault.ts:153-163, but the real parser (capture-auto-save/parse-markdown-to-blocks.ts) is structurally incapable of producing Ok([]): empty input is hardcoded to return `[paragraph("")]` (line 71-73) and the post-loop fallback explicitly appends `paragraph("")` if blocks is empty (line 154-156). This means the spec example 'whitespace-only body' does NOT actually trigger Ok([]); it produces multiple `paragraph("")` blocks (one per line after split). The PROP-029 test in step2-block-parse.test.ts:402-516 only passes because it injects a custom stub `makeBlockParserReturnsEmptyFor` that simulates Ok([]). The end-to-end claim 'whitespace-only body folds to block-parse' (REQ-017 Edge Case) is therefore unverified against the real parser. The actual whitespace-only body behavior is determined by the hydrateNote post-filter (FIND-031), not the Step-2 Ok([]) guard.
recommended_remediation: Either (a) update the real parseMarkdownToBlocks to return Ok([]) when input is whitespace-only / contains no significant content (matching spec REQ-017 example); add a Tier-2 test that calls the real parser with `"   \n   \n   "` and asserts Ok([]) — then the existing PROP-029 stubbed test transitively covers production behavior; or (b) drop the Ok([]) example from REQ-017 / PROP-029 and replace it with a clearly-stubbed-only verification claim, documenting that production never produces Ok([]) and the guard is purely defensive.
